summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his experience attending Edward Tufte's one-day course on presenting data and information in San Francisco. He explains that the course fee includes all four of Tufte's books because Tufte uses them as high-resolution handouts rather than relying on projectors. Despite having read the books before, Atwood found value in Tufte's guided commentary that revealed nuances he had missed. The post culminates in sharing Tufte's extensive reading list and resource recommendations for analytical design education, plus a curated list of 20th-century classics in information architecture.
key_points: Tufte uses his own books as high-resolution handouts during the course, avoiding PowerPoint and projectors for most content
The course includes all four Tufte books, making the net cost only about $130 after group discounts when factoring in their Amazon price
Despite his dry writing, Tufte is a funny and animated speaker who brings out nuances in the text that readers miss on their own
Tufte provides an extensive curated reading list spanning typography, design, cartography, data visualization, and statistics
The post catalogs Tufte's picks for 20th-century information architecture classics, from the Hertzsprung-Russell Diagram to the Internet itself
money_quotes: He uses the books as high-resolution handouts.
Edward Tufte is a funny, animated speaker.
There's nothing in the course you couldn't get directly from the books yourself, but the material is always more effective when it's presented by a good teacher.
Strunk and White, The Elements of Style, the last chapter on style (read once a year).

The quality of interface design may have peaked around 1990; too many computer screens today are overrun with operating system imperialism, featuritis, marketeering banners, and overproduced gimmicks.
The greatest dispersion and intensification of information since the printing of books with moveable type in Korea and China around 650.
key_insight: The best teachers don't just present information—they guide you through material in ways that reveal nuances you'd miss reading alone, which is why high-resolution physical artifacts often beat digital presentations.
